chosen:
Rationale: I will separate (i) “ordinary” algebraic matrix multiplication improvements, which affect APSP only through reductions to ordinary products for bounded weights, from (ii) genuinely min-plus algebra, where APSP is subcubic-equivalent to min-plus product and conjectural barriers apply. I will first locate the relevant APSP/min-plus literature, then cross-check the provided lower-bound candidate for the style of fine-grained barriers.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"bounded integer weights APSP min-plus product ordinary matrix multiplication Zwick bridging sets Alon Galil Margalit all pairs shortest paths small integer weights"}
```
[result] simulated: Search identifies the classical algorithmic line: Alon–Galil–Margalit on APSP via witnesses and matrix multiplication; Shoshan–Zwick on APSP with integer weights; Zwick’s bridging-sets/rectangular-matrix-multiplication framework; and later refinements using the current ordinary matrix multiplication exponent \(\omega\). Key fact recovered: min-plus products with entries in a small integer range can be reduced to a bounded number of ordinary ring products by encoding weights as exponents, giving running times of the form \(\widetilde O(W n^\omega)\) or related rectangular-MM bounds, depending on the precise weight model.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4309132721","focus":"fine-grained lower-bound style for graph distance problems and relation to additive-combinatorial/min-plus barriers"}
```
[result] simulated: The paper is not directly about exact APSP over small integer weights; it concerns stronger 3SUM-based lower bounds for approximate distance oracles. It is useful mainly as contextual evidence that graph-distance data structures often inherit fine-grained barriers from algebraic/additive problems. For exact APSP, the more central barrier is the min-plus product/APSP conjectural equivalence rather than 3SUM.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"min-plus product conjecture APSP equivalence truly subcubic bounded weights small integer weights"}
```
[result] simulated: Search recovers the standard fine-grained picture: exact APSP in directed graphs with polynomially bounded integer weights is subcubic-equivalent, up to polylogarithmic/randomized reductions, to min-plus matrix multiplication. The min-plus product conjecture asserts that no \(O(n^{3-\varepsilon})\) algorithm exists for arbitrary \(n\times n\) min-plus product with polynomially bounded integer entries. This conjecture rules out a general truly subcubic exact APSP algorithm for polynomial-weight directed graphs, but it does not preclude faster algorithms under additional structure such as constant or very small edge weights, bounded differences, planarity, sparsity, or approximation.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Recent improvements in ordinary algebraic matrix multiplication, i.e. reductions in the exponent \\(\\omega\\), transfer to exact APSP over small integer weights only through reductions of bounded-range min-plus operations to ordinary ring products. For matrices with entries in a range of size \\(W\\), the standard encoding \\(a\\mapsto x^a\\) converts a bounded min-plus product into coefficient/exponent extraction from ordinary products, yielding algorithms of the schematic form \\(\\widetilde O(W n^\\omega)\\), with variants using rectangular matrix multiplication and bridging sets. Thus, if edge weights are constant or sufficiently small, current algebraic matrix multiplication gives genuinely subcubic exact APSP, roughly \\(\\widetilde O(W n^\\omega)\\) or comparable bounds.","niche_genome":"The positive result applies to directed or undirected APSP instances whose integer weights are bounded by a small parameter \\(W\\), especially constant or slowly growing \\(W\\). It is not a generic consequence of faster ordinary matrix multiplication for arbitrary weighted APSP, because shortest-path distances may range over \\(\\Theta(nW)\\) and the relevant closure computation is min-plus rather than ring-linear.","observation_genome":"The algorithmic improvement from recent algebraic techniques is therefore mostly parametric: replacing older values of \\(\\omega\\) by the current best ordinary matrix multiplication exponent marginally improves the exponent in bounded-weight APSP algorithms. However, no known purely algebraic improvement to ordinary matrix multiplication collapses general min-plus product to ordinary multiplication with only polylogarithmic overhead.","limitation_genome":"The min-plus product conjecture, and the fine-grained equivalence between exact APSP and min-plus matrix multiplication for polynomially bounded integer weights, forms the main barrier. If arbitrary min-plus product requires \\(n^{3-o(1)}\\) time, then exact APSP with general polynomial integer weights also cannot admit a truly subcubic algorithm. This barrier does not contradict subcubic algorithms for constant-weight or otherwise structured cases, because those instances avoid the full expressive power of arbitrary min-plus matrices.","delta_genome":"The key distinction is between small edge weights and arbitrary min-plus matrix entries. Bounded edge weights permit algebraic speedups because local weight values are drawn from a small alphabet, while arbitrary min-plus product allows independent polynomial-size entries and encodes the full APSP-hard operation. Hence recent algebraic matrix multiplication advances improve the bounded-weight regime quantitatively but do not change the qualitative conjectural boundary for general exact APSP.","claim_genome":"Recent algebraic matrix multiplication techniques yield improved exact APSP algorithms for sufficiently small integer weights only in the bounded-range/structured regime, with running times parameterized by \\(W\\) and \\(\\omega\\), e.g. \\(\\widetilde O(W n^\\omega)\\)-type bounds. Under the standard min-plus product conjecture, these techniques cannot be expected to produce a truly subcubic exact APSP algorithm for arbitrary polynomially bounded integer weights; any such algorithm would imply a truly subcubic algorithm for general min-plus matrix multiplication and would refute the conjecture."}}

full_prompt: Explore the use of 2D materials as selective membranes for carbon capture, emphasizing defect engineering, permeability-selectivity tradeoffs, and manufacturability.
chosen:
I’ll first scan the recent literature landscape to identify recurring membrane bottlenecks and promising 2D-material strategies, then refine toward a concrete research plan.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"2D materials selective membranes carbon capture defect engineering permeability selectivity manufacturability graphene oxide MXene MoS2 CO2 N2"}
```
[result]: Recent work clusters around: (1) graphene/graphene oxide laminates with tunable interlayer galleries but swelling and scale-up issues; (2) nanoporous single-layer graphene with high theoretical permeance but difficult pore-size uniformity and crack-free transfer; (3) MXene and MoS2 membranes with surface chemistry/defect control for CO2-philic transport; (4) mixed-matrix or supported ultrathin films to improve manufacturability. Key gap: linking defect chemistry and pore-size distributions to realistic flue-gas CO2/N2 separation under humidity while preserving scalable fabrication.

To narrow the proposal, I’ll inspect the most actionable direction: defect-engineered 2D laminates rather than pristine monolayer nanopores, because laminates are more manufacturable.  
```action
{"tool":"read","query":"review defect engineered graphene oxide MXene membranes CO2 N2 separation humidity scalable fabrication"}
```
[result]: Reviews emphasize that laminate membranes can be solution-cast, vacuum-filtered, spray-coated, or roll-to-roll coated on porous supports. Selectivity arises from angstrom-scale interlayer spacing, oxygen/fluorine/hydroxyl surface groups, and molecular sieving plus CO2 adsorption. Main limitations are thickness-dependent permeance loss, interlayer swelling in humid feeds, aging/compaction, and defect heterogeneity. Crosslinking, pillaring, ion intercalation, and mild plasma/ozone etching are cited as ways to stabilize channels and introduce CO2-selective sites.

full_prompt: 请撰写自由文本研究提案，探讨在自动驾驶系统中使用形式化验证方法保证感知-决策模块安全性的可能路径。
chosen:
我将先检索自动驾驶形式化验证、运行时监控与感知-决策安全保证相关研究，以确定可行的研究切入点。重点关注感知不确定性如何被抽象为可验证对象，以及决策模块如何与形式规约连接。

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"formal verification autonomous driving perception planning safety runtime monitoring neural network verification"}
```

[result]: 检索到若干相关方向：1）神经网络感知模型的鲁棒性验证与抽象解释；2）基于时序逻辑的自动驾驶行为规约，如 LTL/STL；3）面向规划与控制的可达性分析和模型检测；4）运行时监控与安全壳架构，用于在感知或决策不确定时触发保守策略；5）端到端自动驾驶系统验证仍受限于环境复杂性、感知误差建模和组合状态爆炸。

基于检索结果，我进一步读取一类代表性思路：将学习型感知模块与符号化决策模块解耦，并通过不确定性边界、形式规约和运行时验证建立端到端安全论证。

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"survey on formal methods for autonomous driving perception planning runtime assurance"}
```

[result]: 阅读结果显示，当前可行路径通常不是直接证明完整自动驾驶系统“绝对安全”，而是构建分层保证：对感知模块验证局部鲁棒性和误差边界；对决策模块验证满足交通规则、碰撞避免和可达性约束；在系统运行时使用监控器检查关键安全性质，并在违反风险出现前切换到最小风险策略。研究挑战包括开放世界感知、长尾场景、神经网络不可解释性、形式规约与真实交通语义之间的鸿沟。


full_prompt: Optimize a high-entropy alloy for turbine applications; if the initial path targets higher room-temperature hardness, pivot if creep tests reveal high-temperature phase instability as the primary failure mode. Propose characterization and compositional adjustments to stabilize the desired phases under service conditions.
chosen:
Initial rationale: I’ll start by optimizing for higher room-temperature hardness, assuming hardness is a useful proxy for turbine wear resistance and will correlate with strengthening under service. The first compositional direction would be to increase Al/Ti/Nb/Ta-driven precipitate hardening and possibly some refractory content.
```action search
query: "high entropy alloy turbine creep high temperature phase instability hardness primary failure mode"
```

```result
Evidence indicates that room-temperature hardness alone is not a reliable optimization target for turbine HEAs. In turbine-relevant regimes, creep resistance and phase stability dominate: metastable BCC/B2 or excessive refractory/TCP-forming additions can cause high-temperature decomposition, sigma/mu/Laves formation, precipitate coarsening, embrittlement, and accelerated creep damage.
```

Actually, this changes things: the initial hardness-first path depends on the assumption that stronger-at-room-temperature microstructures remain stable in service. The evidence contradicts that assumption—high-temperature phase instability is the primary failure mode. I should pivot from “maximize RT hardness” to “stabilize creep-resistant phases under turbine temperatures.”

```action read
source: "HEA/superalloy design principles for high-temperature creep stability"
```

```result
For turbine-relevant alloys, desirable microstructures are thermodynamically stable FCC γ matrices with coherent L1₂ γ′ precipitates, controlled grain boundaries, slow diffusion, oxidation resistance, and suppression of brittle TCP/BCC/sigma phases during long-term aging and creep.
```

```json
    "characterization_plan": "Run CALPHAD phase-fraction and Scheil/aging simulations, then validate with high-temperature XRD, DSC/DTA, SEM-EDS, EBSD, TEM/STEM, and atom-probe tomography after long-term aging and creep exposure. The method is well suited to single-agent planning or centralized trajectory generation, but it does not directly address decentralized multi-robot observations, communication delay, or delay-robust inter-robot safety constraints.
```

full_prompt: How can Indigenous ecological knowledge and satellite-based remote sensing be combined to improve wildfire risk prediction and land stewardship practices?
chosen:
To design a strong interdisciplinary plan, I’ll first look for work connecting Indigenous fire stewardship, cultural burning, and remote-sensing wildfire prediction. This helps identify where current models underuse community-held ecological indicators.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Indigenous ecological knowledge satellite remote sensing wildfire risk prediction cultural burning land stewardship"}
```

[result]: Recent literature highlights that Indigenous fire stewardship can improve fuel management, biodiversity, and landscape resilience, while satellite products such as Landsat, Sentinel-2, MODIS, VIIRS, GEDI, and SMAP are widely used to map vegetation condition, fuel dryness, burn severity, canopy structure, soil moisture, and active fires. Key gap: most wildfire risk models rely on biophysical variables but rarely integrate Indigenous ecological indicators such as culturally significant species condition, seasonal burning calendars, traditional fuel classifications, wind/topography knowledge, and place-based fire histories.

I’ll read a representative source on combining community knowledge with geospatial fire management to shape an ethical and practical implementation pathway.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"community-based participatory mapping Indigenous fire knowledge remote sensing wildfire management"}
```

[result]: Best practices emphasize Indigenous data sovereignty, co-designed research questions, participatory GIS, local control over sensitive cultural data, and model outputs that support community decision-making rather than extract knowledge. Successful approaches combine satellite-derived fuel and moisture indices with Indigenous-led field validation, cultural burn planning, and stewardship priorities.

full_prompt: Investigate whether ultralight dark matter fields could produce detectable frequency shifts in next-generation optical atomic clocks. Model the expected signal correlations across a global clock network and identify parameter regions not yet excluded by astrophysical constraints.
chosen:
I’ll first map the recent theory/experiment literature around ultralight scalar dark matter and clock-comparison signals, prioritizing papers that connect couplings to frequency-ratio observables and networks. This should identify both signal models and existing exclusions to avoid proposing something already done.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "ultralight scalar dark matter optical atomic clocks frequency shifts global network constraints", "year_min": 2014, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Found several relevant OpenAlex-indexed works, including W2106679985 “Direct detection of ultralight dark matter with atomic clocks,” W2741809809 “Search for transient variations of the fine structure constant and dark matter using fiber-linked optical atomic clocks,” W2963851531 “New bounds on dark matter coupling from a global network of optical atomic clocks,” W4387220332 “Search for ultralight dark matter with spectroscopy of radio-frequency atomic transitions,” and W4233976554 “Atomic clocks and constraints on variations of fundamental constants.”  

I’ll read the foundational atomic-clock proposal to extract the coupling-to-clock-frequency formalism and the expected coherent oscillatory signature. This is the baseline needed to generalize to next-generation optical clocks.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2106679985"}
```  
[result]: The paper models bosonic ultralight dark matter as a classical oscillating field with frequency set by its mass, producing sinusoidal variations in constants such as α and fermion masses. Clock-ratio shifts are written as linear combinations of sensitivity coefficients times fractional constant variations, with coherence time set by the virial velocity dispersion and spatial coherence length scaling as \(1/(m_\phi v)\).  

Next I’ll read the network-based experimental constraint paper, since the user specifically asks for global clock-network correlations and remaining parameter space. This will help distinguish what current searches do from what a next-generation network could add.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2963851531"}
```  
[result]: This work combines geographically separated optical clocks to constrain transient topological-defect-like dark matter and coupling-induced frequency excursions, emphasizing matched timing, inter-lab comparison, and common-mode rejection. It shows that network analyses gain sensitivity from correlated signatures, but existing limits are mainly for available clock stabilities, limited uptime, and specific transient assumptions rather than a full stochastic/oscillatory scalar-field correlation model.  

I’ll check whether the particular mechanism of a phase-coherent but spatially correlated ultralight field searched through cross-spectral correlations of next-generation optical-clock ratios has been explicitly covered recently. This guards against rediscovering an already-standard analysis.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "cross-spectral correlation search for virialized ultralight scalar dark matter using a global network of next-generation optical atomic clocks, including finite coherence time and spatial phase correlations", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```  
[result]: Related searches exist for single-clock-pair oscillations, transient defects, and GPS/microwave-clock networks, but there is no widely established end-to-end forecast combining next-generation optical-clock noise, Earth-scale spatial correlations, realistic network cadence, and astrophysical-prior masking in the scalar-coupling parameter space. The proposed correlation-statistic and forecast study appears incrementally novel, especially if it treats both coherent sinusoidal and stochastic-field regimes.  

Given the papers and novelty check, I’ll now formulate a focused research plan: derive the frequency-ratio response, simulate the global covariance of the dark-matter field and clock noise, build a detection statistic, and overlay laboratory plus astrophysical exclusions to identify reachable open regions.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose to develop a forecast and analysis framework for detecting ultralight scalar dark matter with a global network of next-generation optical atomic clocks. The central model will treat the dark matter as a virialized classical field, \\(\\phi(t,\\mathbf{x})\\), whose oscillation frequency is set by the particle mass and whose finite velocity dispersion determines the coherence time and spatial coherence length. Its couplings to Standard Model parameters, especially the fine-structure constant, electron mass, and light-quark/QCD scales, will be mapped into fractional shifts of optical clock-frequency ratios using published sensitivity coefficients for Sr, Yb, Hg, Al+, and highly charged ion candidates. The first deliverable will be a transparent signal model: for each clock pair, a predicted time series containing a narrow-band oscillatory component with stochastic phase diffusion, amplitude statistics from the local dark-matter density, and coupling-dependent response coefficients.\n\nThe main new element will be a network-correlation treatment rather than a collection of independent clock-pair searches. For an Earth-scale array, the scalar field is nearly common-mode at sufficiently low masses, but develops baseline-dependent phase decorrelation as the de Broglie wavelength approaches terrestrial distances; meanwhile clock noise, environmental systematics, fiber-link noise, and satellite-transfer noise have different spatial and spectral covariance structures. I will construct a cross-spectral likelihood or matched-filter statistic that combines auto- and cross-correlations among clock ratios, explicitly including finite observing cadence, duty cycle, dead time, clock instability, and heterogeneous species sensitivities. This should identify when a global network improves sensitivity through coherent phase addition, when it instead benefits from stochastic cross-correlation, and when single-site species diversity is more valuable than geographic separation.\n\nA second component will be a parameter-space synthesis. For each scalar mass and coupling combination, I will compare projected clock-network reach against existing exclusions from equivalence-principle tests, fifth-force searches, stellar cooling, black-hole superradiance where applicable, big-bang nucleosynthesis/CMB limits on varying constants, and current laboratory clock bounds. Rather than treating astrophysical limits as a monolithic exclusion, the study will separate robust constraints from model-dependent ones, for example those depending on the scalar’s relic abundance, self-interactions, environmental screening, or whether it constitutes all of the local dark matter. The output will be reach plots for dilaton-like couplings and for single-coupling benchmarks, highlighting mass windows where next-generation optical clocks can test parameter regions not cleanly excluded by astrophysics.\n\nThe expected outcome is a practical observing strategy for international clock networks: which clock species to pair, what baseline lengths matter for different masses, what cadence and campaign duration optimize sensitivity, and which correlated environmental channels must be monitored. A successful analysis would show whether optical clocks can move beyond existing oscillation searches by exploiting spatial correlations and by targeting parameter regions where astrophysical bounds are uncertain or absent. The project is feasible as a theory-and-data-analysis study using published clock sensitivities and projected instabilities, with later application to real data from optical fiber-linked and satellite-linked clock comparisons."}
```

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose a general research project to investigate whether ultralight scalar dark matter could be detected using a global network of next-generation optical atomic clocks. The idea is to look for tiny time-dependent changes in clock-frequency ratios that might arise if dark matter causes fundamental constants to vary. A worldwide network of clocks could compare signals across different locations and clock species to search for correlated patterns.

The project would develop a general model for how scalar dark matter affects atomic clocks, simulate possible signals in future clock networks, and estimate what kinds of dark-matter parameter space might be tested. ```
```

[result]: The search retrieved several mechanistic review candidates relevant to ICI heterogeneity rather than NSCLC-specific prediction models. The strongest anchors were tumor microenvironment remodeling and drug response (`oa:W3080773873`), DNA damage repair and genomic instability (`oa:W3177914501`), natural killer cell biology (`oa:W3046958019`), aging-related immune biology (`oa:W4311744984`), and lactate metabolism/immunosuppression (`oa:W4294031309`). These sources suggest that response prediction should integrate tumor-intrinsic genomics, host immune state, metabolic context, and non-biologic access-related determinants.

I’ll read the tumor microenvironment review first because differential response to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade in NSCLC is often mediated by immune-cell composition, stromal exclusion, suppressive myeloid cells, cytokines, and metabolic constraints.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3080773873"}
```

[result]: The paper emphasizes that the tumor microenvironment is a dynamic system composed of immune cells, stromal cells, extracellular matrix, cytokines, vasculature, and metabolic products. It highlights that these components can either support immune infiltration and therapy response or create immune-excluded and immunosuppressive niches. For NSCLC ICI response, this supports including determinants such as PD-L1 expression, T-cell inflamed phenotype, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, tumor-associated macrophage polarization, hypoxia, lactate-rich metabolism, and prior therapy-induced TME remodeling.

Next I’ll read the DNA damage repair review to capture genetic determinants such as tumor mutational burden, neoantigen generation, mismatch repair defects, and DNA repair pathway alterations, which are central to ICI sensitivity but not always sufficient predictors.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "oa:W3177914501"}
```

[result]: The review frames genomic instability and DNA damage repair defects as key sources of mutation accumulation and tumor evolution. It links defects in repair pathways to higher mutational load, altered antigenicity, and potential therapeutic vulnerability. For advanced NSCLC, this supports modeling response around tumor mutational burden, smoking-associated mutational signatures, POLE/POLD1 or mismatch repair defects when present, STK11/KEAP1-associated resistance patterns, antigen-presentation machinery, and HLA diversity, while recognizing that genomic predictors interact strongly with immune contexture and host factors.

Because the prompt explicitly asks about clinical, genetic, and social determinants, I’ll check whether the proposed integrated mechanism—linking tumor immunogenicity, host clinical vulnerability, and structural access factors to ICI response—is novel enough as a framing rather than just a restatement of known biomarkers.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Integrated prediction of immune checkpoint inhibitor response in advanced NSCLC using interacting clinical determinants, tumor genomic immunogenicity and resistance features, tumor microenvironment state, and social determinants of care access/adherence", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025}
```

[result]: Existing literature commonly evaluates PD-L1 expression, tumor mutational burden, STK11/KEAP1/KRAS status, ECOG performance status, smoking history, steroid exposure, antibiotic exposure, comorbidity, and inflammatory indices as separate or partially integrated predictors. Fewer studies explicitly combine these with social determinants such as insurance status, neighborhood deprivation, transportation barriers, rurality, race/ethnicity-mediated care inequities, treatment delays, and trial access. The framing is therefore not novel at the individual-biomarker level, but is relatively novel as a multi-domain causal prediction agenda that treats social determinants as modifiers of biological benefit rather than merely background covariates.

I’ll synthesize the findings into a structured research proposal genome. The goal is to answer the academic question by proposing a clinically usable, multi-domain prediction model for differential ICI response in advanced NSCLC.

2. Sociology and media-industry studies on automation, platformization, deskilling/reskilling, freelance precarity, and copyright conflicts in cultural labor.  
3. Literary studies debates on authorship, originality, intertextuality, genre fiction, fan fiction, and the changing status of the “author” when writing becomes collaborative with machines.  
4. Publishing-industry reports discussing AI-assisted editing, marketing copy, audiobook narration, translation, rights management, and concerns from authors’ guilds and editorial workers.

To ground the proposal, I’ll inspect one representative source cluster on cultural labor and generative AI in publishing.

[result]: Simulated reading summary: The literature suggests that generative AI is not simply replacing authors but redistributing creative agency across authors, editors, platforms, datasets, and model providers. In publishing, AI tools are being adopted unevenly: large firms experiment with workflow automation, while freelancers and genre writers often encounter AI through productivity pressure and market competition. Key unresolved issues include attribution, consent for training data, copyright ownership, editorial deskilling, the visibility of human labor, and whether AI-generated or AI-assisted texts alter literary value.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project investigates how generative AI tools are reshaping authorship, creativity, and labor in the publishing industry by combining methods from computer science, sociology, and literary studies. Its central research question is: when AI systems participate in writing, editing, marketing, translation, and narration, how are creative agency and professional responsibility redistributed among human authors, publishing workers, platforms, and machine-learning systems? Rather than treating AI as either a neutral tool or an autonomous creator, the project will analyze publishing as a sociotechnical system in which literary production is increasingly mediated by proprietary models, datasets, interfaces, and workplace policies.\n\nThe project will use a mixed-methods design. From computer science, it will examine the affordances and limits of generative AI systems used for drafting, copyediting, summarization, cover copy, translation, and metadata generation, with attention to provenance, bias, style imitation, and detectability. From sociology, it will conduct interviews with authors, editors, literary agents, translators, audiobook workers, and publishing managers to understand how AI adoption changes workflows, job security, professional identity, and bargaining power. From literary studies, it will analyze AI-assisted texts and public controversies around them in relation to older theories of authorship, originality, intertextuality, genre convention, and the death or persistence of the author.\n\nA key contribution will be to distinguish several emerging models of AI-mediated authorship: AI as assistant, AI as ghostwriter, AI as editor, AI as market analyst, and AI as synthetic competitor. Each model implies different ethical and legal questions. For example, AI as assistant may intensify existing creative labor while leaving the author’s name intact; AI as ghostwriter challenges attribution and transparency; AI as market analyst may push literary production toward data-driven sameness; and AI as synthetic competitor may affect wages and visibility for freelance writers, translators, and narrators. The project will therefore connect textual analysis with labor analysis, showing that debates over creativity cannot be separated from ownership, compensation, and control over infrastructure.\n\nThe expected outcome is an interdisciplinary account of generative AI in publishing that moves beyond simple claims of replacement or innovation. The project will produce a typology of AI-assisted publishing labor, a set of case studies of changing authorship practices, and policy recommendations for transparency, credit, consent, and worker protection. Ultimately, it argues that generative AI is not ending authorship but making authorship more distributed, contested, and dependent on hidden technical and human infrastructures. Understanding this transformation requires reading AI-generated texts not only as literary artifacts, but also as evidence of changing labor relations in contemporary cultural production."}
```

I first search within the requested 2018–2025 window to anchor the comparison in recent genomics, association-modeling, and functional-adaptation literature.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"comparative genomics functional assays predictive modeling genes adaptation comparative 2018 2025","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```

[result]: The search returns the provided OpenAlex candidates, with three most relevant methodological anchors: oa:W3159728495 for high-quality comparative genomics resources, oa:W3195285310 for GWAS/predictive association approaches, and oa:W3010798294 for functional mechanisms of plant salt adaptation. oa:W4387344289 is useful as a genetics-to-proteomics predictive extension, while oa:W3040245690 is less central because it concerns microbiome definitions rather than adaptation-gene discovery.

Next I read the three core papers together to establish the empirical and conceptual basis for the comparison: genome resources, predictive association studies, and functional stress-response biology.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W3159728495; oa:W3195285310; oa:W3010798294"}
```

[result]: oa:W3159728495 emphasizes that near-complete, error-free assemblies enable reliable cross-species comparisons, synteny inference, structural-variant detection, and lineage-specific gene-family analysis. oa:W3195285310 frames GWAS as a statistical approach linking genomic variants to measured phenotypes across many individuals, with strengths in scale but limitations around causality, population structure, and missing heritability. oa:W3010798294 reviews salt-tolerance mechanisms in plants, highlighting ion transport, osmotic adjustment, signaling, ROS homeostasis, and physiological assays that can validate candidate adaptive genes under controlled stress.

I extract methodological “genomes” from the three papers so that each approach can be compared using the same six dimensions.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"oa:W3159728495; oa:W3195285310; oa:W3010798294"}
```

[result]: Comparative genomics genome: mechanism = infer adaptation genes from conserved/divergent sequences, structural variants, gene-family changes, and signatures of selection enabled by high-quality assemblies; niche = non-model and vertebrate-scale biodiversity comparisons; observation = orthology, synteny, assembly completeness, lineage-specific change; limitation = correlation with adaptation is often indirect without phenotype or assay validation; delta = better assemblies reduce false positives; claim = complete genomes improve discovery of candidate adaptive loci. Predictive modeling/GWAS genome: mechanism = statistically associate variants with traits across individuals; niche = populations with genotypes and phenotypes; observation = SNP/variant effect sizes and polygenic scores; limitation = confounding, small effects, causality gaps; delta = scalable prioritization of candidate loci; claim = association models identify genomic regions linked to adaptive phenotypes. Functional-assay genome: mechanism = perturb, expose, or measure biological response under adaptive stress; niche = experimentally tractable organisms, tissues, or pathways; observation = expression, fitness, physiology, ion flux, growth, survival; limitation = lower throughput and context dependence; delta = causal validation; claim = functional evidence distinguishes true adaptive genes from correlated candidates.

I compare comparative genomics against predictive modeling because both can scan genome-wide, but they differ sharply in whether evidence is evolutionary or phenotype-association based.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"oa:W3159728495","proposed_genome":{"mechanism":"GWAS/predictive modeling links variants to adaptive traits using population-scale genotype-phenotype associations rather than cross-species divergence.","niche":"Within-species or closely related population datasets with measured phenotypes, environmental covariates, or fitness proxies.","observation":"Variant-trait associations, effect sizes, polygenic architecture, risk or trait prediction performance.","limitation":"Associations may be non-causal because of linkage disequilibrium, population stratification, unmeasured environments, and weak power for rare variants.","delta":"Adds direct phenotype linkage to candidate genes that comparative genomics can only nominate indirectly.","claim":"Predictive association models prioritize adaptation genes when adaptive phenotypes are measurable across many genotyped individuals."}}
```

[result]: The main difference is evidentiary direction: comparative genomics begins from evolutionary conservation/divergence, while GWAS begins from trait variation. Comparative genomics is stronger for deep evolutionary hypotheses and non-model species once assemblies are available, whereas predictive modeling is stronger for population-level trait mapping. A hybrid would use comparative signals as priors in association models to reduce the candidate space and improve biological interpretability.

I then compare comparative genomics against functional assays, because this is the key contrast between discovery-by-pattern and validation-by-mechanism.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"oa:W3159728495","proposed_genome":{"mechanism":"Functional assays test candidate adaptation genes by measuring biological response after stress exposure, perturbation, expression change, or physiological manipulation.","niche":"Controlled laboratory, greenhouse, cell, organismal, or field-assay systems where adaptive stressors such as salinity, heat, drought, pathogens, or hypoxia can be imposed.","observation":"Growth, survival, ion balance, osmolyte accumulation, transcript/protein abundance, mutant phenotype, rescue, or fitness under stress.","limitation":"Lower throughput, species-specific protocols, artificial assay conditions, and difficulty capturing long-term ecological adaptation.","delta":"Converts candidate-gene lists into causal or mechanistic evidence.","claim":"Functional assays are best suited to validating whether nominated genes actually contribute to adaptive performance."}}
```

[result]: Comparative genomics is high-throughput and hypothesis-generating, while functional assays are lower-throughput but closer to causal inference. The largest complementarity is that comparative genomics can nominate many candidates across lineages, whereas assays can rank or falsify them under relevant adaptive conditions. The risk is that laboratory assays may validate stress response rather than natural adaptation unless ecological context is included.

Finally I compare predictive modeling with functional assays, since many adaptation studies fail at the transition from statistical association to biological mechanism.

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","parent_id":"oa:W3195285310","proposed_genome":{"mechanism":"Functional validation tests whether statistically predicted genes or variants alter organismal, cellular, or molecular performance under adaptive conditions.","niche":"Follow-up experiments after GWAS, genomic prediction, environmental association, or machine-learning prioritization.","observation":"Perturbation effects, expression response, physiological performance, fitness proxies, pathway activation, and genotype-by-environment effects.","limitation":"Experimental validation is slower and may not scale to thousands of model-nominated loci; effects can be background- or environment-dependent.","delta":"Resolves the causality gap left by predictive models.","claim":"The strongest evidence arises when prediction identifies candidates and functional assays demonstrate adaptive effect."}}
```

[result]: Predictive modeling offers breadth and quantitative prioritization, but functional assays provide depth and causal credibility. Their most productive relationship is sequential: model first to prioritize loci, then assay top candidates under ecologically meaningful stressors. The combination reduces both false-positive statistical associations and unscalable experimental screening.

Before proposing a synthesis, I check whether the mechanism of “triangulated candidate discovery” across comparative genomics, predictive association, and functional validation is already a common claimed framework in the recent literature.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","mechanism":"triangulated adaptation-gene discovery using comparative genomics as evolutionary prior, predictive genotype-phenotype modeling as prioritization layer, and functional assays as causal validation","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025}
```

[result]: The novelty check finds many studies combining two components, especially comparative genomics plus functional validation or GWAS plus functional follow-up. Fewer frameworks explicitly define a three-layer workflow in which comparative genomics supplies evolutionary priors, predictive modeling ranks candidates, and functional assays close the causal loop. The mechanism is therefore not wholly novel in ingredients, but the unified comparison and staged hybrid design are plausibly useful and under-formalized.

full_prompt: Design a research concept using bird wing morphing mechanisms to improve the agility and energy efficiency of fixed-wing drones. How might feather-inspired variable geometry enable stable flight across changing wind conditions?
chosen:
Rationale: First, search the biology literature for how birds achieve variable wing geometry, especially feather-mediated morphing during gusts, turns, and speed changes.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "bird wing morphing feather spreading covert feathers alula gust response variable geometry flight stability"}
```

[result]  
Found biology literature on avian wing morphing showing that birds vary wing span, sweep, camber, twist, and feather spacing during maneuvering and gust rejection. Key recurring mechanisms include:  
- **Alula deployment** for delaying stall at high angles of attack.  
- **Primary feather spreading and rotation** for passive load alleviation and vortex control.  
- **Wing folding/sweep changes** for speed-agility tradeoffs.  
- **Feather compliance** enabling local aeroelastic adaptation without full-wing actuation.  

Rationale: Next, search the robotics/aerospace literature for fixed-wing drone morphing and the limitations of current variable-geometry UAV systems.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "fixed wing drone morphing wing variable geometry gust rejection energy efficiency agile flight UAV"}
```

[result]  
Found robotics and UAV literature on morphing wings using variable sweep, camber-changing airfoils, twist actuation, compliant skins, and folding wings. Current systems can improve endurance or maneuverability, but common limitations are:  
- High actuator mass and power consumption.  
- Slow morphing response relative to gust timescales.  
- Structural complexity and reliability issues.  
- Limited local adaptation; most systems morph the whole wing rather than feather-scale aerodynamic elements.  
- Tradeoff between stability in turbulence and agility during aggressive maneuvers.  

Rationale: Extract the transferable “genome” from the biological field: what principle from bird wings is most useful for drones?

```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "field": "biology", "sources": ["avian wing morphing", "alula deployment", "primary feather spreading", "passive feather compliance"], "focus": "mechanisms transferable to fixed-wing drones"}
```

[result]  
Biology genome extracted:  
- **Mechanism:** Feather-inspired distributed variable geometry.  
- **Core principle:** Birds do not rely only on global wing shape change; they use many semi-independent feather elements that passively or actively adjust local flow.  
- **Functional effects:** Stall delay, gust load alleviation, drag modulation, lift redistribution, roll/yaw control, and stable flight across variable wind.  
- **Most transferable concept:** A hybrid system combining passive compliant feather-like trailing/leading-edge elements with sparse active control for larger morphing states.  

Rationale: Compare this biological mechanism against robotics gaps to identify the cross-domain contribution.

```action
{"tool": "genome_diff", "field_A": "biology: feather-mediated distributed morphing", "field_B": "robotics: fixed-wing UAV morphing and gust rejection", "objective": "identify novel proposal space"}
```

[result]  
Genome difference identified:  
- Biology uses **distributed, feather-scale, partially passive control surfaces**.  
- Robotics often uses **centralized, rigid, actuator-heavy morphing mechanisms**.  
- Opportunity: develop a fixed-wing drone wing with feather-inspired variable geometry: overlapping compliant vanes or featherlets that can passively splay under gust loading and actively tune spanwise lift distribution.  
- Hypothesis space: such wings could improve agility and energy efficiency by reducing actuator burden while maintaining aerodynamic adaptability.  

Rationale: Check whether the proposed idea is distinguishable from existing morphing-wing UAV work.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "query": "feather inspired distributed morphing wing UAV passive active featherlets gust rejection energy efficient agile fixed wing drone"}
```

[result]  
Novelty check result: partially related work exists in bio-inspired morphing wings, artificial feathers, compliant trailing edges, and bird-like drones. However, the distinct contribution would be:  
- A **feather-inspired variable-geometry wing architecture** for fixed-wing drones that combines passive gust-responsive featherlets with active low-bandwidth morphing control.  
- A control strategy that treats featherlet deflection as both an aerodynamic actuator and a wind-sensing signal.  
- Evaluation across changing wind conditions, measuring agility, energy use, and stability rather than only lift/drag improvement.  

Rationale: Synthesize the cross-disciplinary proposal into a research concept.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Feather-mediated distributed wing morphing from birds, especially primary feather spreading, passive feather compliance, and alula-like stall-delay deployment",
    "niche_genome": "Fixed-wing drones lose agility and energy efficiency in changing wind because conventional wings have limited local flow adaptation and morphing mechanisms are often actuator-heavy",
    "observation_genome": "Birds maintain stable, efficient, and agile flight in gusts by continuously changing wing geometry at multiple scales, including local feather-level adjustments that robotics has not fully exploited",
    "limitation_genome": "Drone-scale morphing must be lightweight, low-power, fast enough for gust response, structurally robust, and controllable without adding excessive sensing or actuation complexity",
    "delta_genome": "Design a fixed-wing drone with overlapping feather-inspired variable-geometry elements along the leading and trailing edges. These featherlets passively splay, twist, or open under gust loads while sparse micro-actuators tune their baseline stiffness and deployment angle. The system enables local lift redistribution, stall delay, drag modulation, and gust load alleviation without requiring full-wing morphing",
    "claim_genome": "Compared with a conventional rigid-wing drone of equal span and mass, the feather-inspired morphing wing will reduce gust-induced roll/pitch deviations by at least 25%, reduce control energy by at least 15%, and improve loiter endurance by at least 10% in variable-wind flight tests while maintaining or improving turn-rate performance"
  }
}
```

[result]  
Research concept generated: **Feather-inspired variable-geometry wings for agile, energy-efficient fixed-wing drones.**  
The project borrows bird feather morphing as a distributed aerodynamic control principle and applies it to UAV wing design. The central idea is that stable flight across changing wind conditions can be achieved not only by faster electronic control, but by embedding passive and semi-active aerodynamic intelligence into the wing itself.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"第1天杭州飞昆明，抵达后住昆明市区，下午去翠湖、云南陆军讲武堂感受老昆明氛围，傍晚到滇池海埂大坝或西山看日落，晚餐推荐过桥米线、汽锅鸡或野生菌火锅。第2天上午可游云南民族村或斗南花市，下午乘动车前往大理，约2小时；晚上住大理古城，逛人民路、洋人街，吃喜洲粑粑、乳扇、白族酸辣鱼。\n\n第3天大理环洱海，建议包车或租车/拼车走洱海生态廊道、才村、喜洲古镇、海舌公园附近、双廊，重点拍摄喜洲稻田、白族民居、洱海日落；不建议全天骑电动车环海，距离较长且较累。第4天上午可去崇圣寺三塔或苍山感通索道，下午动车/拼车到丽江，晚上游丽江古城，拍大水车、四方街夜景，晚餐推荐腊排骨火锅、鸡豆凉粉、纳西烤鱼。\n\n第5天丽江自然风光日，安排玉龙雪山、蓝月谷、甘海子一线；若想登冰川公园需提前预约索道并注意高反，摄影重点是雪山倒影、蓝月谷湖水和牦牛草甸。第6天从丽江出发经虎跳峡前往香格里拉，途中游虎跳峡上虎跳或中虎跳视体力选择，下午抵达独克宗古城，转世界最大转经筒，晚餐吃牦牛肉火锅、青稞饼，住香格里拉古城附近。\n\n第7天香格里拉深度游，上午游松赞林寺，拍藏式建筑与湖面倒影；下午去纳帕海或普达措国家公园，偏摄影和草原风光可选纳帕海，偏森林湖泊徒步可选普达措。第8天根据航班安排返程：若香格里拉有合适航班可香格里拉飞昆明/杭州；若机票贵，可早上汽车返回丽江，再从丽江飞杭州。返程日尽量不要安排高强度景点，预留交通缓冲。\n\n预算按人均控制在7000元以内：杭州往返云南机票提前订约1500-2200元；7晚住宿选择舒适客栈/经济酒店约1400-2000元；城际动车、汽车、包车拼车约900-1300元；门票及索道约800-1200元；餐饮约1000-1300元；其他购物和保险约300-500元，总计约5900-7200元。若想稳控预算，建议香格里拉段选择拼车、玉龙雪山只去蓝月谷或提前抢索道票、住宿避开古城核心景观房。\n\n交通建议采用杭州飞昆明、昆明动车到大理、大理动车到丽江、丽江拼车/商务车到香格里拉的顺行路线；行李不宜过多，因为古城石板路拖箱不便。注意云南昼夜温差大、紫外线强，需带防晒、墨镜、薄羽绒或冲锋衣；香格里拉海拔较高，前一晚避免饮酒和剧烈运动。摄影上建议带广角和中长焦，洱海、纳帕海适合日出日落，丽江古城适合蓝调时刻，松赞林寺适合上午光线。"}
```

full_prompt: Develop a research concept for carbon-negative construction materials that incorporate industrial waste streams while maintaining structural performance.
chosen:
I’ll first scan the recent landscape to identify promising carbon-negative binder/material systems that can use industrial waste without sacrificing strength and durability.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"carbon-negative construction materials industrial waste streams structural performance geopolymer mineral carbonation steel slag concrete"}
```

[result]: Recent work highlights three converging approaches: (1) alkali-activated/geopolymer binders using fly ash, ground granulated blast furnace slag, and calcined clays; (2) accelerated mineral carbonation of alkaline wastes such as steel slag, cement kiln dust, and waste concrete fines; and (3) biochar or biomass ash additions for long-term carbon storage. Key technical barriers include variability of waste chemistry, durability under freeze-thaw/chloride exposure, embodied carbon verification, and maintaining compressive/flexural strength comparable to ordinary Portland cement concrete.

To refine the concept, I’ll read into mineralized steel-slag and biochar-containing cementitious composites because they directly combine industrial waste utilization with possible net carbon storage.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"review articles and recent studies on CO2-cured steel slag concrete and biochar-modified cementitious composites"}
```

[result]: CO2 curing of steel slag and cementitious wastes can form stable carbonate phases such as calcite and magnesite-like compounds, improving early strength and reducing leachable alkalinity. Biochar can store biogenic carbon and improve internal curing, but excessive loading may reduce workability and strength unless surface-treated or used as a lightweight aggregate/filler. Hybrid systems combining alkali-activated slag/fly ash binders, carbonated steel slag aggregate, and optimized biochar dosages appear promising for structural-grade blocks, panels, and precast elements.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This research concept proposes a carbon-negative structural composite based on three coordinated waste-derived components: an alkali-activated binder made from ground granulated blast furnace slag, Class F fly ash, and cement kiln dust; a mineral-carbonated aggregate fraction produced from steel slag or recycled concrete fines; and a low-dose engineered biochar filler derived from waste biomass. The central hypothesis is that combining mineral carbonation with durable biogenic carbon storage can yield a net carbon-negative construction material while achieving structural performance comparable to conventional precast concrete.\n\nThe project would focus on precast elements such as masonry units, façade panels, pavers, and non-prestressed structural blocks, where controlled curing and quality assurance are practical. Steel slag and recycled concrete fines would be exposed to accelerated CO2 curing before incorporation, converting reactive Ca/Mg phases into stable carbonates and reducing leaching risk. The binder chemistry would be tuned using alkali activation and cement kiln dust alkalinity to reduce or eliminate Portland cement while maintaining early-age strength. Biochar would be surface-treated or mineral-coated to improve bonding with the matrix and used at modest replacement levels to avoid strength loss.\n\nThe experimental program would combine mix design, mechanical testing, durability evaluation, and carbon accounting. Key performance targets would include 28-day compressive strength above 35–50 MPa depending on application, acceptable modulus and flexural strength, low chloride penetration, resistance to freeze-thaw cycling, and controlled shrinkage. Microstructural characterization using XRD, SEM, thermogravimetric analysis, and carbon quantification would determine how carbonate formation, geopolymer/alkali-activated gels, and biochar-matrix interfaces contribute to strength and durability.\n\nA major innovation is the use of waste-stream variability as a design parameter rather than a limitation. The study would develop a mix-design framework that classifies industrial wastes by reactive CaO/MgO, amorphous aluminosilicate content, alkalinity, particle size, and contaminant risk, then recommends processing routes such as carbonation, grinding, washing, or blending. Life-cycle assessment would be integrated from the start to compare each formulation against ordinary Portland cement concrete, accounting for avoided landfill, CO2 uptake during curing, biochar carbon permanence, transport, processing energy, and end-of-life scenarios.\n\nThe expected outcome is a validated pathway for manufacturing carbon-negative structural precast materials from regionally available industrial wastes. If successful, the work would provide both a material formulation and a certification-oriented performance framework, enabling producers to replace high-carbon cementitious products with durable alternatives that permanently store CO2, consume problematic waste streams, and maintain structural reliability."}
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This research concept proposes developing a carbon-negative construction material made from industrial waste streams while maintaining adequate structural performance. The general idea is to combine waste-derived binders, recycled aggregates, and carbon-storing additives into a durable composite that could replace conventional cement-based products in selected building applications.

The project would investigate suitable waste materials, optimize mixture proportions, and evaluate whether the resulting composites can meet practical requirements for strength, durability, workability, and environmental performance. Potential applications could include blocks, panels, pavers, and other precast construction elements.
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","idea_plan":{"title":"适合材料科学初学者切入的新能源电池材料研究方向","directions":[{"name":"钠离子电池正极材料：普鲁士蓝类或层状氧化物","why_worth_studying":"钠资源丰富、成本低，是锂离子电池的重要补充；目前循环稳定性、空气稳定性和倍率性能仍有提升空间。","beginner_entry":"从文献综述入手，比较普鲁士蓝类、层状氧化物、聚阴离子正极的结构特点与性能；进一步可做简单掺杂、包覆或热处理对性能影响的研究。","example_question":"不同金属离子掺杂是否能改善钠离子正极的结构稳定性和循环寿命？"},{"name":"锂离子电池正极材料表面包覆改性","why_worth_studying":"LiFePO4、三元材料等体系成熟，资料丰富；表面副反应、界面阻抗和循环衰减仍是关键问题。","beginner_entry":"选择一种常见正极材料，研究碳包覆、氧化物包覆或磷酸盐包覆对电化学性能的影响。","example_question":"薄层氧化物包覆能否降低正极/电解液界面副反应并提升高倍率性能？"},{"name":"硅基负极体积膨胀缓解","why_worth_studying":"硅负极理论容量高，但充放电过程体积膨胀严重，导致粉化和容量快速衰减。","beginner_entry":"适合做文献分析或材料复合设计，如硅/碳复合、硅氧材料、粘结剂优化等。","example_question":"不同碳基缓冲结构如何影响硅负极循环稳定性？"},{"name":"凝胶/聚合物电解质与安全性提升","why_worth_studying":"电池安全性是产业和学术共同关注的问题；凝胶电解质兼具一定液态电解质导电性和固态电解质安全优势。","beginner_entry":"可从聚合物基体、锂盐种类、增塑剂和无机填料对离子电导率及机械性能的影响入手。","example_question":"加入陶瓷纳米填料是否能同时提升凝胶电解质的离子电导率和热稳定性？"},{"name":"锂硫电池中多硫化物穿梭效应抑制","why_worth_studying":"锂硫电池理论能量密度高，但多硫化物穿梭导致容量衰减，是经典而清晰的研究问题。","beginner_entry":"可研究多孔碳、金属氧化物、MXene或MOF衍生材料对多硫化物的吸附/催化作用。","example_question":"极性金属化合物修饰隔膜能否减弱多硫化物穿梭并提升循环寿命？"},{"name":"废旧锂离子电池回收与再生材料","why_worth_studying":"电池回收兼具环境意义和产业价值；适合材料科学与可持续发展交叉研究。","beginner_entry":"初学者可从回收工艺综述、正极材料直接再生、金属浸出与沉淀回收等方向切入。","example_question":"直接再生方法相比湿法冶金在能耗和材料性能恢复方面有何优势？"}],"recommended_starting_path":["先选一个电池体系：锂离子、钠离子、锂硫或固态电池。","阅读3–5篇近三年综述，整理核心材料、关键问题和常用表征方法。","选择一个小变量作为课题入口，例如掺杂元素、包覆层、碳材料比例、热处理温度或电解质添加剂。","用结构—性能关系组织研究：材料结构变化如何影响离子传输、界面稳定性和循环寿命。"],"most_beginner_friendly":["钠离子电池正极材料综述与改性","锂离子电池正极表面包覆","硅/碳复合负极文献与实验设计"],"suggested_first_project":"建议从“钠离子电池正极材料的结构稳定性提升”或“锂离子电池正极表面包覆改性”开始，因为这两个方向文献丰富、问题清楚、实验路线相对成熟，适合初学者建立材料合成、结构表征和电化学性能分析的基本科研框架。"}}
```

```action search

```

[Result] The targeted check confirms that Dell-style spreading activation remains a standard computational account of speech-error production, while interactive alignment remains a major account of dialogue adaptation. Recent work still tends to treat production errors and conversational alignment as separable phenomena, leaving room for a metric that predicts how interaction context shifts semantic versus phonological error likelihood.

Rationale: The study should combine lexical-semantic activation dynamics with partner-driven alignment pressure, then test whether that joint state predicts error type during interactive speech.

```action propose
    "core_hypothesis": "Interactive alignment modulates production-error patterns by increasing activation for recently shared semantic and lexical representations. ```
```

full_prompt:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- Deep Generative Models (DGMs): Methods such as VAE, GAN, normalizing flows, and energy-based models generate molecules by learning the distribution of known actives. While data-driven and capable of end-to-end optimization, they often produce molecules close to the training set, limiting novelty and optimization capability (e.g., JTVAE, Gen3D).
- Combinatorial Optimization Methods: Genetic algorithms (GA), Bayesian optimization, MCMC, and reinforcement learning approaches directly search discrete chemical space. GAs such as Autogrow 4.0 and Graph-GA excel in molecular optimization benchmarks, but typically act via random-walk exploration, limiting efficiency and reusability across targets.
- Structure-based SBDD and Tree Search: Classical SBDD methods utilize tree search or evolutionary algorithms, optimizing ligand binding in protein pockets using physical models or force-field-based scoring (e.g., RASSE, CONCEPTS, SPROUT). Recent 3D generative models condition on protein pockets but still closely replicate training data.
- Hybrid Methods: Approaches like GA+D and GEGL integrate neural networks to guide GA steps, but rarely utilize protein structural information or cross-target knowledge transfer, limiting their adaptability for SBDD.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in the research area of molecular generation and optimization stem from the limitations of existing methodologies. First, the reliance on Deep Generative Models (DGMs) often results in generated molecules that closely resemble the training data, limiting novelty and exploration of diverse chemical spaces, which hinders the discovery of unique compounds. Additionally, traditional combinatorial optimization methods, such as Genetic Algorithms (GAs), suffer from inefficiencies due to their random-walk exploration strategies, which do not leverage the structural information of protein targets. This leads to high variance in results and substantial computational costs without guaranteeing effective optimization across different targets. Furthermore, hybrid methods that combine neural networks and GAs frequently overlook the integration of protein structural data and fail to facilitate cross-target knowledge transfer, limiting their adaptability and generalizability. Finally, there is a lack of mechanisms to suppress random exploration behavior in these models, which directly contributes to inefficiencies in the molecular search process. Collectively, these challenges underscore the need for approaches that effectively incorporate structural information, enhance exploration capabilities, and enable knowledge transfer across diverse optimization tasks in structure-based drug design.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Current approaches either ignore protein target structure (traditional GAs, generic generative models) or fail to generalize optimization knowledge across tasks, resulting in inefficient random exploration, high variance, and unnecessary computational cost. Existing methods also often lack mechanisms to suppress random-walk behavior and do not exploit the shared ligand-target binding physics.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
Structure-based drug design stands to benefit tremendously from algorithms that (1) leverage protein target structure, (2) efficiently traverse chemical space, (3) transfer binding knowledge across tasks, and (4) deliver stable, robust performance under resource constraints. Addressing these would advance de novo drug discovery and reduce reliance on brute-force simulation.

###5. Task Objective:
To develop a structure-based de novo drug design algorithm that optimizes ligand binding affinity to given protein targets by intelligently exploring chemical space, leveraging 3D structural information, and transferring binding knowledge across targets for improved efficiency and robustness.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- Deep Generative Models: Learn molecule distributions conditioned on protein pockets, but are biased to training data, resulting in low novelty and limited optimization capability.
- Traditional Genetic Algorithms: Randomly select crossover/mutation actions over populations to optimize docking scores; strong performance but high variance, no knowledge transfer, and fail to use protein structure.
- Structure-Based GAs (e.g., Autogrow 4.0): Use chemical reaction-based mutations for better synthesizability; still rely on random selection and lack intelligent guidance.
- RL-Guided Generative Models: Use policy gradients (e.g., REINVENT, MolDQN) for molecule generation; focus on local modifications and do not utilize 3D structures for global optimization.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)
chosen:
I first map the structure-conditioned generative modeling literature because the proposal must exploit protein geometry without simply cloning pocket-conditioned training examples.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "protein pocket conditioned 3D molecular generation equivariant diffusion Pocket2Mol TargetDiff DiffSBDD structure based drug design", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search returns several anchor papers: Pocket2Mol, OpenAlex `W4288785280`; TargetDiff, OpenAlex `W4386126941`; DiffSBDD, OpenAlex `W4386911639`; and related SE(3)-equivariant pocket-conditioned generation work. The common pattern is direct sampling of 3D molecules in pockets, usually optimized for geometric complementarity and learned likelihood, but with recurring concerns about training-set resemblance, synthetic tractability, and weak transfer as an optimization policy.

Next I search for molecular genetic algorithms and discrete combinatorial baselines, since the challenge statement emphasizes that GAs work well but explore by inefficient random walks.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "graph genetic algorithm molecular optimization GraphGA AutoGrow4 genetic algorithms strong baselines molecule generation", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search identifies Jensen’s graph-based genetic algorithm, OpenAlex `W2913638934`; AutoGrow4, OpenAlex `W3018570727`; and “Genetic algorithms are strong baselines for molecular optimization,” OpenAlex `W2963938803`. These papers collectively show that reaction/graph-level mutation and crossover remain competitive, but most variants are target-agnostic and spend many evaluations on unguided structural perturbations.

I then search specifically for neural-GA hybrids to understand what has already been attempted and where target-structural transfer is missing.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "neural guided genetic algorithm molecular design GEGL GA+D deep learning guided molecular optimization", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: The search surfaces hybrid approaches including Genetic Expert-Guided Learning, OpenAlex `W4290895778`, and neural discriminator- or predictor-guided evolutionary loops related to GA+D. These methods generally learn from molecular properties or expert preferences, but rarely condition the evolutionary operator itself on 3D protein-pocket structure or reuse learned search operators across protein families.

I read the graph-based GA paper because it is the cleanest representative of discrete molecular evolution without protein context.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2913638934"}
```  
[result]: Jensen’s graph-based GA encodes molecules as graphs and applies chemically valid mutation/crossover to optimize benchmark objectives, showing that simple evolutionary search can outperform several deep generative baselines on constrained optimization tasks. Its strength is robust exploitation of molecular edits, but its weakness for SBDD is that edits are not informed by binding-site geometry, pharmacophoric constraints, or target-family priors.

I read AutoGrow4 because it represents a practical SBDD evolutionary system using docking and fragment operations.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W3018570727"}
```  
[result]: AutoGrow4 uses genetic operations, reaction rules, docking, and filtering to evolve ligands in a target pocket. It is structurally aware through scoring, but the proposal distribution is still largely hand-designed and stochastic, so the method can require many docking calls and does not amortize lessons learned from previous targets.

I read Pocket2Mol to capture the strengths and weaknesses of pocket-conditioned generative models.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W4288785280"}
```  
[result]: Pocket2Mol incrementally generates atoms conditioned on 3D protein-pocket context, using geometric deep learning to produce ligands compatible with local binding environments. It demonstrates that protein structure can guide generation, but generation remains a one-shot learned sampler rather than a reusable search policy that can adaptively improve candidates under noisy docking and medicinal-chemistry objectives.

I read TargetDiff because diffusion models are now a leading 3D SBDD paradigm and define the strongest generative-model baseline for the proposed work.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W4386126941"}
```  
[result]: TargetDiff formulates pocket-conditioned ligand generation as equivariant diffusion over atom types and coordinates, improving 3D realism and binding-site compatibility. However, diffusion sampling can still inherit dataset biases, often lacks explicit synthetic edit trajectories, and does not naturally suppress unproductive random exploration during downstream optimization.

Having identified the gap—GAs optimize well but wander, while pocket-conditioned DGMs know protein geometry but are weak adaptive optimizers—I propose a structured mini-review and research program around transferable, structure-conditioned evolutionary operators.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Develop Target-Conditioned Evolutionary Transport, a hybrid SBDD framework in which an SE(3)-equivariant protein-pocket encoder controls the mutation, crossover, and fragment-addition operators of a molecular genetic algorithm. Instead of using neural models only as scorers or one-shot generators, the model learns a reusable edit policy: given a ligand, pocket, docking pose, interaction map, and optimization history, it proposes chemically valid edits expected to improve binding, novelty, and developability. The edit policy is trained by offline imitation from successful GA/docking trajectories across many targets, then refined by off-policy reinforcement learning using docking-score deltas, interaction recovery, strain penalties, synthetic accessibility, and uncertainty-aware exploration. A memory-based anti-random-walk module penalizes edits that repeatedly fail in similar pocket microenvironments and encourages directed moves such as growing into unoccupied hydrophobic subpockets, installing hinge-binders, replacing solvent-exposed liabilities, or improving shape complementarity.", "niche_genome": "The niche is structure-based molecular optimization for targets where one has a protein pocket but limited target-specific ligand data. The proposal sits between classical SBDD evolutionary search, which uses docking but weak learned priors, and 3D generative models, which use protein geometry but tend to be dataset-shaped samplers. It is especially relevant for kinase subfamilies, proteases, GPCR pockets, and emerging targets where transfer from related pockets could reduce expensive docking/evaluation cycles.", "observation_genome": "The reviewed literature suggests three converging observations. First, graph and reaction-based GAs remain highly competitive because they perform explicit molecular editing and can directly optimize arbitrary black-box objectives. Second, pocket-conditioned equivariant models such as Pocket2Mol and TargetDiff encode useful geometric information but are not designed as iterative, target-adaptive optimization policies. Third, current hybrids usually guide selection or scoring rather than learning the actual structural edit distribution that turns a mediocre docked ligand into a better one.", "limitation_genome": "Existing GAs suffer from high-variance random exploration, many wasted docking calls, and limited cross-target memory. Existing DGMs risk generating molecules close to training ligands and often provide weak guarantees on synthesizability, docking improvement, or novelty under iterative optimization. Hybrid methods underuse 3D protein structure and rarely learn transferable knowledge such as which fragment edits work for which pocket motifs. The proposed approach must also address docking-score noise, pose instability, synthetic feasibility, and possible overfitting to benchmark protein families.", "delta_genome": "The empirical study will compare against GraphGA, AutoGrow4, Pocket2Mol, TargetDiff, and neural-guided GA baselines on CrossDocked-style pocket splits, scaffold splits, and target-family leave-one-out transfer. Primary metrics will include docking improvement per evaluation, hit rate under a docking threshold, scaffold novelty, Bemis-Murcko diversity, synthetic accessibility, pose-interaction consistency, and prospective enrichment in retrospective targets. Key ablations will remove the pocket encoder, remove trajectory imitation, replace learned mutation with random mutation, remove failed-edit memory, and test zero-shot versus few-shot transfer to unseen protein families.", "claim_genome": "The central claim is that protein-conditioned evolutionary operators can combine the optimization power of GAs with the structural awareness of 3D DGMs while avoiding the main failure modes of both. If successful, the method should reduce docking evaluations, produce more novel but chemically feasible scaffolds, and transfer search knowledge across related targets. The broader contribution is a shift from molecule generation as static sampling to SBDD as amortized, structure-aware search: learning not only what ligands look like, but which chemical edits are productive in which protein microenvironments."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed review/proposal genome completed. The resulting research program is novel relative to the surveyed work because the learned object is not merely a molecule generator or property predictor, but a transferable, pocket-conditioned evolutionary edit policy designed to suppress random GA behavior while preserving discrete chemical optimization.

full_prompt: Some recent work suggests that demand response can replace a large share of peaking generation, but the assumptions about user behavior seem optimistic. How can we test whether this claim remains valid in more realistic settings?
chosen:
I start by reading an anchor paper with a strong, policy-relevant claim: flexible demand can substitute for a substantial amount of peaking capacity. I want the exact behavioral and reliability assumptions before looking for failures or replications.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W2964088836"}
```
[result]: The anchor study models residential/commercial demand response as a dispatchable capacity resource in a high-renewables grid and finds large reductions in gas peaker utilization and capacity needs. Its core result depends on high enrollment, low event opt-out, fast load recovery, and aggregator control that is treated as dependable during scarcity hours. The paper acknowledges that elasticity and participation are drawn from pilot-scale or program-average evidence rather than from repeated critical-peak events under heat-wave stress.

Next I search specifically for replication, validation, or field-trial evidence on whether demand response actually delivers dependable peak capacity. The concern is not whether DR can shift load once, but whether it behaves like firm capacity across correlated system-stress events.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"replication validation demand response peaking capacity reliability field trial opt out critical peak pricing","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: The search returns field and quasi-experimental studies of critical peak pricing, smart thermostat demand response, and emergency DR programs. Several sources report statistically significant reductions in peak load, but the magnitude varies strongly with weather, baseline methods, customer fatigue, and rebound. The most relevant papers include a large-scale smart-thermostat DR evaluation, a review of residential DR persistence, and a capacity-accreditation paper emphasizing uncertainty and coincidence with peak risk.

I read a field-evaluation paper because it tests the main behavioral assumption directly: do enrolled customers provide load relief during real peak events? This is the closest evidence to a replication of the anchor model’s behavioral module.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3015924217"}
```
[result]: The field study estimates causal peak reductions from thermostat-based residential demand response during summer events. It finds meaningful average reductions, but the distribution is skewed: a minority of homes deliver most savings, reductions degrade during long or repeated events, and rebound occurs shortly after control periods. The paper also notes that enrolled households are not representative of the full population, implying that scaling the program could lower average per-customer response.

Because the anchor claim is system-level, I look for criticism from resource-adequacy or capacity-credit literature rather than only end-use experiments. The key question is whether DR should be counted as firm replacement capacity for peakers.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"criticism demand response capacity credit resource adequacy peaking generation uncertainty rebound opt out","year_min":2018,"year_max":2025,"k":5}
```
[result]: The search finds papers arguing that demand response capacity value is sensitive to event duration, call limits, baseline manipulation, customer heterogeneity, and coincidence with loss-of-load hours. A recurring criticism is that energy-shifting DR may reduce net peak in normal years but underperform during extreme heat waves when air-conditioning loads and customer discomfort are highest. Several studies recommend probabilistic capacity accreditation rather than deterministic derating.

I read a resource-adequacy paper that explicitly models demand response uncertainty and compares it with conventional capacity. This helps translate behavioral doubt into reliability metrics like ELCC or loss-of-load expectation.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W3168262184"}
```
[result]: The paper shows that DR’s effective load-carrying capability can be much lower than its nameplate enrolled capacity when event availability, duration constraints, rebound, and correlated nonresponse are modeled. It argues that treating DR as fully dispatchable exaggerates replacement of peaking plants, especially in systems where scarcity is driven by multi-hour heat events. The authors recommend stress-tested accreditation using weather-correlated performance distributions rather than average event impacts.

I now read an adjacent-failure source: even when DR works technically, social and program-design frictions can erode response over time. This checks whether the anchor paper’s high participation and persistence assumptions are optimistic.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W4285902035"}
```
[result]: This review synthesizes recent residential DR trials and finds persistent gaps between technical potential and realized flexibility. Common limitations include low recruitment, attrition, override behavior, comfort constraints, rebound peaks, and equity concerns when automation or enabling technology is unevenly distributed. The review concludes that DR is valuable but should be modeled as a stochastic, saturating, and behaviorally mediated resource rather than a fully controllable generator substitute.

Before proposing the test, I extract the anchor paper’s “genome” so the validation targets the exact causal chain: technology plus participation assumptions imply avoided peakers. I will alter only the behavioral realism assumptions while preserving the same grid and technology setting.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"https://openalex.org/W2964088836"}
```
[result]: Extracted mechanism: aggregator-controlled flexible loads reduce net peak and reserve requirements, allowing retirement or non-build of peaking generators. Niche: power-system planning under high renewable penetration with residential/commercial demand response. Observation: modeled DR portfolio displaces a large share of peaker capacity while maintaining reliability. Limitation: behavioral response is parameterized using optimistic or average participation, availability, and event-performance assumptions.

The validation experiment should be a reproduction-with-behavioral-stress-test: rerun the anchor capacity-expansion/resource-adequacy model, replacing deterministic DR with empirically estimated stochastic response from field trials. The decisive outcome is whether peaker displacement survives under realistic heterogeneity, attrition, rebound, and extreme-weather correlation.
